case_name: Pediatrics Cool Care, Jose J. Salguero, M.D., PA, Jose J. Salguero, M.D., and Jenelle Robinson, P.A.-C. v. Ginger Thompson, Individually and as the Representative of the Estate of A.W.(Deceased), and Brad Washington

summary.brief: Pediatrics Cool Care, Jose J. Salguero, M.D., PA, Jose J. Salguero, M.D., and Jenelle Robinson, P.A.-C. v. Ginger Thompson, Individually and as the Representative of the Estate of A.W.(Deceased), and Brad Washington. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: In 2010, Ginger Thompson’s (“Thompson”) and Brad Washington’s (“Washington”) daughter, A.W., who was twelve years old, began receiving treatment as a patient at Pediatrics Cool Care (“PCC”), a full-service pediatric clinic located in Ennis, Texas.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On July 31, 2012, Thompson called PCC and asked for a refill of A.W.’s Celexa prescription. At that time, Bernadette Aguillon (“Aguillon”), a medical assistant at PCC, spoke to Thompson and told her that she would call in a prescription for a 30-day supply and three refills.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On August 14, 2012, A.W. was found unresponsive in her room and later pronounced dead. An autopsy documented her cause of death as suicide from a Benadryl overdose.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: This is an appeal from a jury verdict in a medical malpractice and wrongful death action brought by appellees, Ginger Thompson, individually and as the representative of the estate of A.W. (deceased), and Brad Washington against appellants Pediatrics Cool Care, Jose J. Salguero, M.D., P.A., Jose J. Salguero, M.D., and Jenelle Robinson, P.A.-C., resulting in a judgment in favor of appellees.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Appellees elicited expert testimony from Herschel Robert Lessin, M.D. (“Dr. Lessin”), a board-certified pediatrician, regarding the standard of care for the healthcare providers at PCC, and expert testimony from Fred Moss, M.D. (“Dr. Moss”), a board-certified psychiatrist, regarding causation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On May 22, 2018, the jury returned a verdict in favor of appellees, answering question 1 “Yes” as to whether the negligence of Dr. Salguero, M.D. and Robinson proximately caused the death of A.W., but “No” as to Kawalek.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 1, 2012, A.W. went to PCC with her mother and was evaluated by Robinson. At that time, A.W. complained of depression, stating that she felt sad all the time, that she could not control her feelings, that she cried while watching television commercials, and that she was stressed out.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Robinson spent less than thirty minutes evaluating A.W. Robinson did not perform a detailed psychiatric work-up on A.W. or use the depression questionnaire that was available; she diagnosed A.W. with depressive disorder and prescribed a 30-day supply of Celexa (20mg/day).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Without Robinson’s knowledge, a medical assistant at PCC changed A.W.’s medical record from a diagnosis by Robinson of depression to “depression, not otherwise specified.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury awarded damages of $7,635,000.00 in favor of appellees against appellants.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We consider the legal-sufficiency challenge first. See Windrum v. Kareh, 581 S.W.3d 761, 777–79 (Tex. 2019). When reviewing the legal sufficiency of the evidence, we consider the evidence in the light most favorable to the challenged finding and indulge every reasonable inference that would supports it.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Because we have overruled all issues on appeal, we affirm the judgment of the trial court.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: Family history of depression and bipolar disorder

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient reports feeling sad all the time, inability to control feelings, crying during TV commercials, and feeling stressed. Depression ongoing for some time.
medications: None documented prior to Celexa prescription

critical_findings: Family history of depression and bipolar disorder

requestables.description: Detailed psychiatric work-up and use of depression questionnaire

reveal.decision_impact: Missed opportunity to assess severity and risk of suicidal ideation

options.description: Conduct a detailed psychiatric evaluation and refer to a mental health specialist

options.description: Prescribe Celexa without detailed evaluation or specialist referral
options.clinical_reasoning: Inadequate assessment and lack of specialist referral

expected_action_court.description: Conduct a detailed psychiatric evaluation and refer to a mental health specialist

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to conduct a detailed psychiatric evaluation and refer to a specialist

explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony
